title: GICS Sector P/E Ratios 2026: Which Sectors Are Overvalued?
description: Half the sectors trade above their 10-year average P/E — but which ones? See how every GICS sector compares to its own historical norm.

schemas.headline: GICS Sector P/E Ratios 2026: Which Sectors Are Overvalued?
schemas.description: Average P/E ratio for all 11 GICS sectors with historical comparison. Find which sectors are overvalued relative to their 10-year average.

mainEntity.name: Which stock market sector has the highest P/E ratio?

acceptedAnswer.text: As of March 2026, Communication Services has the highest trailing P/E ratio at 37.47, driven by mega-cap platforms like Alphabet and Meta. Information Technology follows at 34.96, and Real Estate is third at 32.72. However, when adjusted for growth (PEG ratio), Technology actually appears more reasonably valued at 1.34 PEG vs Communication Services at 2.44.

The Information Technology sector trades at a trailing P/E of 34.96 — a 27% premium to its 10-year average of 27.5. However, the sector's PEG ratio of 1.34 (the lowest of all sectors) suggests the elevated P/E is partly justified by exceptional 33% 5-year earnings growth. The forward P/E of 24.96 narrows the gap further. The sector is expensive on an absolute basis but appears reasonably priced when growth-adjusted.

mainEntity.name: What is the average P/E ratio of the S&P 500?

acceptedAnswer.text: The S&P 500 trailing P/E ratio is 28.95 as of March 2026. The historical mean P/E is 16.20 and the median is 15.06, meaning the current market trades at roughly 79% above its long-term average. Elevated earnings growth expectations and mega-cap tech concentration contribute to this above-average multiple.

mainEntity.name: Which sector is the most undervalued?

acceptedAnswer.text: Real Estate is the only GICS sector trading below its 10-year average P/E, at a 12% discount (32.72 current vs 37.0 historical). Financials offer the lowest absolute P/E at 16.92 and the second-lowest PEG at 1.44. Energy is closest to fair value at only a 7% premium, while Materials has the second-lowest PEG at 1.40 thanks to strong forward growth expectations.

mainEntity.name: How do you compare sector valuations?

acceptedAnswer.text: Comparing sector P/E ratios requires context. Key approaches: (1) Compare current P/E to the sector's own 10-year historical average to assess relative premium/discount. (2) Use PEG ratio (P/E divided by earnings growth rate) to adjust for different growth profiles — a PEG below 1.0 suggests undervaluation. (3) Compare trailing vs forward P/E to see if earnings are expected to improve. (4) Consider P/B and P/S ratios for capital-intensive vs asset-light sectors. No single metric tells the full story.
